# UC-22.16.5 · DMZ jump host concurrent multi-segment sessions

## Description

Supports pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "DMZ jump host concurrent multi-segment sessions".

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for TSA SD compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes linux and sourcetypes linux_secure. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=linux`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Industrial Asset Intelligence, Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (4295), Splunk Edge Hub, Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)..
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `pan:traffic`, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, `nozomi:alert`, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=linux sourcetype=linux_secure host="jmp-pipeline-dmz-*" earliest=-7d
| eval uc_tag="22.16.5"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)Accepted\\s+\\w+\\s+for\\s+(?<user>\\S+)\\s+from\\s+(?<src>\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+)"
| transaction user maxspan=1800s
| eval multi_host=if(mvcount(mvdedup(host))>1,1,0)
| where multi_host=1
```

Understanding this SPL

**DMZ jump host concurrent multi-segment sessions (TSA architecture)** — Supports pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "DMZ jump host concurrent multi-segment sessions".

Documented **Data sources**: `pan:traffic`, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, `nozomi:alert`, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Industrial Asset Intelligence, Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (4295), Splunk Edge Hub, Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: linux; **sourcetype**: linux_secure; **host** filter: jmp-pipeline-dmz-*. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=linux, sourcetype=linux_secure, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_tag** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• Groups related events into transactions — prefer `maxspan`/`maxpause`/`maxevents` for bounded memory.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **multi_host** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where multi_host=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP or perimeter firewall log (for example Palo Alto or FortiGate) for the same window. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart, Choropleth or site bar, Table, Single value

## SPL

```spl
index=linux sourcetype=linux_secure host="jmp-pipeline-dmz-*" earliest=-7d
| eval uc_tag="22.16.5"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)Accepted\\s+\\w+\\s+for\\s+(?<user>\\S+)\\s+from\\s+(?<src>\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+)"
| transaction user maxspan=1800s
| eval multi_host=if(mvcount(mvdedup(host))>1,1,0)
| where multi_host=1
```
